title: En el restaurante
description: Ordering food, polite expressions, and using ser/estar with food vocabulary.

cards.notes: Conditional of *querer*. The polite version of *Quiero* (I want), which sounds blunt in service contexts. Treat as a fixed phrase at A1.
Goes at the end of the request, like English: *Un café, por favor*. Don't overuse — Spanish is direct; one *por favor* per request is enough.
Intensifier: *muchas gracias* (thanks very much). Reply: *de nada* (you're welcome — lit. *of nothing*).
The standard way to ask for the check. *La cuenta* uses *la* — feminine noun. Don't confuse with *el cuento* (the story) — different word.
Same role as French *Bon appétit*. Said by staff serving food AND by companions before eating. Always with exclamation marks.

Tricky exception: *agua* is **feminine** (ends in -a), but takes ***el*** in the singular to avoid the harsh *la a-* sound. Becomes *las aguas* (regular) in the plural and *agua fría* (regular feminine agreement) for adjectives. Same trick: *el águila* (the eagle), *el hambre* (the hunger).
Distinguish from *el vinto* (doesn't exist — common typo). *Un vaso de vino* (a glass of wine) or *una copa de vino* (a wine glass — for stemmed glasses).
Default order in Spain is a *café solo* (espresso). Other common types: *café con leche* (with milk), *café cortado* (espresso with a splash of milk).
Specifically fish-as-food (after being caught). Live fish is *el pez* (plural *los peces*). Menu always uses *pescado*.

Uses ***estar*** — describing the food's CURRENT state (it tastes good as you eat it). *Es bueno* (with ser) would mean *the food is good for you / nutritious* — totally different meaning. Classic ser/estar trap, perfect chance to revise Lesson 04.

steps.title: Eating out in Spain (or anywhere Spanish-speaking)
steps.body:
# En el restaurante

Spanish-speaking restaurant culture has its own rhythm — and the politeness patterns are slightly different from what English speakers expect.

This lesson covers:
- **Polite ordering phrases**: *quisiera*, *por favor*, *gracias*
- **The bill** and **wishing someone a good meal**
- **Common menu vocabulary**
- **A bonus** ser/estar revision with food (*Está bueno* vs *Es bueno*)

Cultural notes:
- Service is included; tipping is optional (5-10% if service was great).
- **¡Buen provecho!** is said before eating, by waiters AND by people sharing a table.
- *Café* defaults to espresso. Specify if you want something else.
- *Agua del grifo* (tap water) is usually free; *agua mineral* (bottled) is charged.

steps.title: Polite ordering
steps.body:
## The polite request pattern

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| Quisiera ___ | I would like ___ |
| ___, por favor | ___, please |
| Gracias / Muchas gracias | Thanks / Thanks a lot |
| De nada | You're welcome |

**Quisiera** is the conditional of *querer*. At A1 you can treat it as a fixed polite formula. Pair it with the article + noun:

- *Quisiera **un café**, por favor.*
- *Quisiera **una ensalada**, por favor.*
- *Quisiera **el menú**, por favor.* (the menu — specific one)

Note: *Quiero* (I want) is grammatically correct but sounds blunt to service staff. Stick with *Quisiera* in restaurants.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I would like a coffee, please.*

steps.title: Menu basics
steps.body:
## Food and drink vocabulary

| Spanish | English | Note |
|---|---|---|
| el pan | bread | m. |
| **el** agua | water | *el* despite being f. — see below |
| el vino | wine | m. |
| el café | coffee | m. |
| el pollo | chicken | m. |
| el pescado | fish (food) | m. — live fish is *el pez* |
| la ensalada | salad | f. |
| el queso | cheese | m. |

**The *agua* trick**: *agua* is feminine (*agua fría* — cold water, feminine agreement on the adjective), but takes ***el*** in the singular to avoid the harsh *la a-a-* sound. The same trick applies to other feminine nouns that START with a stressed -a sound: *el águila* (the eagle), *el hambre* (the hunger).

In the plural it returns to feminine: *las aguas frías* (the cold waters).

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish menu word with its English meaning.
How do you write *the water* in Spanish?
exercise.hint: Feminine noun but takes *el* in the singular because it starts with a stressed -a.

steps.title: Bonus — ser vs estar with food
steps.body:
## ¡Está bueno! vs Es bueno

Food is the classic battleground for *ser* vs *estar*. The contrast is sharp:

- ***El pescado es bueno.*** — Fish is good (for you, nutritionally — *general* statement). This is the dietary-advice meaning. Identity / general truth → **ser**.
- ***El pescado está bueno.*** — The fish is good / tasty (RIGHT NOW, on this plate, you're eating it). State / opinion in the moment → **estar**.

If you say *El vino es bueno*, you sound like you're writing a sommelier review (*Wine is a good drink*). If you say *El vino está bueno*, you're complimenting the wine you're drinking RIGHT NOW.

The shortcut: when you want to compliment the food at the table, use ***está***, not *es*. Even native English speakers learn this contrast slowly; recognising it is half the work.

exercise.prompt: You're at the table, you taste the soup, and it's great. You say…
exercise.hint: Current state, this specific soup → estar. Soup (*sopa*) is feminine → buena (feminine).
Definite article + noun + *por favor*. *La cuenta* — feminine.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to ask: *I would like a glass of water, please.*
exercise.hint: *Quisiera* + *un vaso de agua* + *por favor*. Note: *agua* drops the article (no *el*) after *de*.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish phrase with when you'd use it.
Arrange to mean: *I would like a salad and the fish, please.*
exercise.hint: Indefinite *una* for salad (any salad), definite *el* for the fish (specific menu item).
